title: Greece plans green fund to accelerate shipping’s decarbonization
summary: Greece's new "Green Fund" will recycle FuelEU Maritime emission fines, euphemistically labelled "green resources," back into the industry. While ostensibly supporting decarbonization and port infrastructure, this scheme risks becoming another greenwashing initiative without immediate, drastic cuts to carbon intensity. dirtiestships.com demands tangible results: a significant reduction in actual emissions, not just studies or future promises.
content:
As reported, the Green Fund, which will receive the revenues generated through FuelEU Maritime and will operate under the supervision of the Ministry of Maritime and Insular Policy, will establish five main funding priorities aimed at supporting the decarbonisation of shipping and the development of modern port infrastructure.

Eligible beneficiaries of the relevant programmes will include shipping companies, port authorities, universities and research centres involved in the field of energy transition.

According to Article 117 of the draft legislation, FuelEU revenues will be classified as “green resources” and allocated to the Green Fund through a dedicated account maintained exclusively to serve the objectives of Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 under the Ministry of Maritime and Insular Policy.

At the same time, Article 120 outlines the priority areas where these funds will be directed.

Furthermore, under the provision, the collected resources will support initiatives aimed at decarbonising shipping and facilitating the transition of the Greek fleet toward cleaner energy sources.

Eligible actions include supporting the use of renewable and low-carbon maritime fuels, developing and testing innovative technologies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and implementing these solutions across the Greek fleet.

Particular emphasis is also placed on port infrastructure, with funding foreseen for the construction and upgrade of alternative fuel bunkering facilities, as well as onshore power supply (OPS) infrastructure for vessels at Greek ports.

The funds may also be used for the installation of technologies that improve vessel energy efficiency and contribute to reducing greenhouse gas emissions, as well as for technical, environmental and economic studies or the provision of specialised advisory and support services.

The draft legislation also stipulates that up to 2% of the previous year’s revenues may be allocated to cover the operating expenses of the Green Fund, which will manage the related resources.
